title: Generator expressions vs list comprehensions

project_usage: aggregating metrics without loading all into memory

overview.what: A generator expression looks just like a list comprehension, but uses parentheses `()` instead of square brackets `[]`. Instead of building an entire list in memory all at once, a generator expression uses lazy evaluation. It calculates and yields exactly one item at a time, pausing execution until the next item is requested. This means a generator expression takes up virtually no memory, regardless of how many items it will eventually produce. You can use them directly inside aggregation functions like `sum()`, `max()`, or `any()`.
overview.why: When processing millions of database rows or gigabytes of log files, loading the entire dataset into memory with a list comprehension will crash your application with an OutOfMemory error. Generator expressions allow you to process massive datasets in a constant O(1) memory footprint.
where_used: Aggregating data directly (e.g. `sum(x for x in data)`)

why_learning_this: Understanding lazy evaluation and memory efficiency
Knowing when to trade the random-access capability of lists for the memory savings of generators
Understanding why a generator appears 'empty' if you try to iterate over it twice

common_mistakes.title: Iterating a generator twice
common_mistakes.explanation: A generator expression is exhausted as it produces items. If you loop over it a second time, it yields nothing because all items have already been consumed. If you need multiple passes, use a list.

common_mistakes.title: Trying to index or slice
common_mistakes.explanation: Generators only know how to produce the *next* item. You cannot access `gen[5]` or check its `len()` because the items don't exist yet.

recall_questions.q: What is the primary difference in memory usage between a list comprehension and a generator expression?
recall_questions.answer: A list comprehension allocates memory for the entire resulting list at once. A generator expression yields one item at a time (lazy evaluation), taking up minimal memory regardless of the total item count.

recall_questions.q: What syntax difference separates a list comprehension from a generator expression?
recall_questions.answer: A list comprehension is enclosed in square brackets `[]`, while a generator expression is enclosed in parentheses `()`.

recall_questions.q: What happens if you try to loop over the same generator expression twice?
recall_questions.answer: The second loop will execute zero times. A generator is exhausted upon its first traversal; it does not reset.

practice_tasks.title: Memory-efficient sum
practice_tasks.prompt: Change the list comprehension to a generator expression to calculate the sum of squares without allocating memory for the entire list.
practice_tasks.starter_code:
def calculate_sum():
    # Change this line:
    total = sum([x * x for x in range(1, 1001)])
    return total

code_walkthrough.code:
numbers = [1, 2, 3]
gen = (x * 10 for x in numbers)

print(next(gen))
print(next(gen))
print(next(gen))
code_walkthrough.focus: The `next()` function pulls exactly one value out of the generator expression at a time, proving it evaluates lazily.

aha_moment.code:
gen = (x for x in [1, 2, 3])

first_pass = list(gen)
second_pass = list(gen)

print(second_pass)
aha_moment.why: A generator expression is consumed as it is read. The `first_pass` exhausted the generator. When `second_pass` tried to read from it, there were no items left, returning an empty list.

challenge.title: Any big numbers?
challenge.prompt: Using a generator expression with the `any()` function, check if there are any numbers greater than 100 in `nums`. Assign the boolean result to `has_big`.
challenge.solution:
nums = [10, 50, 99, 101, 2]
has_big = any(x > 100 for x in nums)

understanding_checks.question: What is the output of the second print statement?
understanding_checks.code:
gen = (x * 2 for x in [1, 2, 3])
print(list(gen))
print(list(gen))
understanding_checks.why: A generator expression is evaluated lazily and exhausted as it is consumed. The first `list(gen)` consumes all items. The second `list(gen)` finds an empty generator.

understanding_checks.question: A junior developer is confused why `my_gen[0]` raises a TypeError when `my_gen` is a generator expression. How do you explain it?
understanding_checks.answer: Generator expressions do not support indexing because their items are evaluated lazily one at a time.
understanding_checks.why: Unlike lists, which hold all their items in memory at once and support random access, generators only know how to produce the next item.
